speaker: interviewer
text: Today is July 22, 1996. I will be interviewing Henry Fuchs at home in Poland. His name was Hesiek Tauber. The interviewer is Tina Tito. We are in Flushing, New York, in the United States. The language will be English. Today is July 2-- 22, 1996. I will be interviewing Henry Fuchs. I am the interviewer, Tina Tito. Mr. Fuchs, can you please tell us your full name and spell it for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry Fuchs. H-E-N-R-Y and name Fuchs, F-U-C-H-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you called at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hesiek.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-E-S-I-E-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your last name at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tauber. T-A-U-B-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you explain why you were called Tauber?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- after my mother's name. She was married to my father, Fuchsbrunner, by a rabbi, [INAUDIBLE], without registering to the-- to the-- to the-- with the registry. So actually, this was a-- in great part, in our town, this was the custom. That's why, officially, my name is Tauber. But everybody knows me as Fuchsbrunner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell us your father's full name, Fuchsbrunner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fuchsbrunner. Here, I write it F-U-C-H-S-B-R-U-N-N-E-R. But in some places, it was written without N-N-E-R but with a M-E-R. Fuchsbrumer. And some other, from the same family, write with an N, Fuchsbruner. So it's-- that's was the-- that's the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: July 8, 1917. But I go here as 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chrzanów, Galicia, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please spell the name of your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: C-H-R-Z-A-N-O-W, O with a-- with a stripe, which means Chrzanow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father born in the same town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was born [SIGHS] in Oklusz. Shall I spell it? O-L-K-U-S-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it near this town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very near. But it was-- this was-- I was Galicia, and he was Congress Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did he move from his town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he moved to-- he didn't move, but he-- he came to Chrzanow at a time, by 1905, there was the Russian-Japanese war. And at that time, [SIGHS] people who didn't want to serve in the army, they looked for a way to-- for some place where to come. So he changed his address, and he came to Chrzanow. And in Chrzanow, he had-- he had family in Chrzanow. His family, his name was-- Tausowitz. [SIGHS] Tausowitz was-- Johann Tausowitz. He was-- he was a veterinarian. And his wife, her name was [? Koshitsky ?] My father's mother's name is [? Koshitsky ?] So actually, it was family. And this is the reason they came to Chrzanow. And he met my mother there. And they got married there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us why he did not want to join the Russian army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I should give you a good answer. i don't have it. But-- [LAUGHS] and I-- this is the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when he was a young man, before he got married-- did he come alone or did he come with family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came alone.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for work in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: He worked in-- in leather manufacturing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did that entail? What did he have to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: The work? He opened up shop, and he made parts of shoes, the-- the soft parts of shoes, which at the time, whoever assembled shoes, he bought the-- the soft part already made. And whatever they had, they made the soles, they put in the soles. And they finished the shoe. And this was his-- this was his profession.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he sell it? Did he sell it privately or to other shops?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he sold it to-- to-- to-- not to shop, to customers, which, they made the shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have a shop just in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he have it just in one town, his place of work? Or in another place as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in Chrzanow, [SIGHS] most part in Chrzanow till the-- in the '30s. So he opened up shop in Cracow, which was wholesale leather, just leather. In Chrzanow later, he gave up also the parts. But he just sold leather to the leather trade. Which other words-- the other ones who made the parts, and the other ones who made the shoes. They bought leather, just leather. And this was for his profession. And then, when my brother-- which is now in Belgium, the older brother of me, with-- with him, he put my brother-- in-- in Cracow, he opened up a wholesale place in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your town then. What was your father's full name in the town, his first name, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Avraham Shmiel Fuchsbrunner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's full name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mindl Tauber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mindl Tauber.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother have a profession?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had-- had a profession. She-- she had a shop. She made shirts. She made-- she made ladies' wears.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she have the shop?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember that. [LAUGHS] I was the youngest in the family. When I [SIGHS] was a kid, she was well-established. And she didn't work no more. But before, when she was single, maybe-- I don't know. Maybe she had one kid, two kids. She still worked. But then she gave it up. She didn't need it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your brothers and sisters. Can you name them for us?

speaker: interviewee
text: My older sister, Golda. Golda Lya. Then came my brother, Bendit. Then came my sister, Rifka Rosa, which is Regina. Then came my brother, Reuven. Then I came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the school that you attended in your hometown. Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In public school-- public school. It was on the-- it was two schools. One-- one school was on the-- [SIGHS] near-- near the-- near the-- the [NON-ENGLISH], the marketplace. And one was on the [NON-ENGLISH]. I lived near the [NON-ENGLISH]. So I went to this school. Most-- most Jewish in Chrzanow went to the other school. Because it was near the-- more-- more-- more near the center of the town. And I-- I was more mixed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your school was mixed, Polish children and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Polish-- Polish more than-- than Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] they called names. They picked the payots. But that's the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you react when they called you names or played with your payots?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't fight back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Back-- it was a time by the end of the year, where relatives came and waited up out of the school, and there was a fight. They-- they-- they-- they-- they did their work, you know, because of provocation. And then we-- we-- we had to [SIGHS] face the same-- same game.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Pulled by the payots, and called names, and that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Obviously, you're--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't have friends amongst the Polish students?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not many.

speaker: interviewer
text: But some.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we kept together. Jews kept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were obviously a very Orthodox Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school?

speaker: interviewer
text: In your-- in your family, in your life.

speaker: interviewee
text: The family was orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you belonged to a certain shtebl?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shtebl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the shtebl have a name? Did it have a particular--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the rabbi of the shtebl?

speaker: interviewee
text: There-- there was no rabbi, but there was a [SPEAKING NON-ENGLISH]. That's what they called them. He was [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] didn't-- didn't [INAUDIBLE] shtebl. He was-- he was a [INAUDIBLE] shtebl, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my town. His name was Halberstam.

speaker: interviewer
text: The man-- the-- the person who ran your shtebl?

speaker: interviewee
text: The person who was a rabbi in Chrzanów was Halberstam. And the [INAUDIBLE], which was in the-- in the Radomsk shtebl was Eisenberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were part of the Radomsk Hasidim?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hasidim, yeah. Radomsk, better saying my-- I was in Radomsk. But there was-- my father was [INAUDIBLE]. He was a-- a rabbi-- it was same Radomsk. But he was a uncle from Radomsk rabbi. In other words, he was a brother from Radomsk rabbi's father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went to-- to this shtebl. [INAUDIBLE] was also in Chrzanów But I was-- I was-- Chrzanów was not [INAUDIBLE]. It was a Radomsk yeshiva. So I studied there. And I belonged there. When-- after-- did you study after school?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time of school, I went to [NON-ENGLISH]. This wasn't the time for yeshiva. When I was school age, we learned from 8:00 till 1:00, and from-- in school, public school, every day. But in the morning, from 6:30 till 7:30, we went to cheder. Then we davened. And we rushed to school 8 o'clock. And we stood there till 1 o'clock. And from 1:00 to 3:00 was lunch, or-- or-- or supper-- better saying supper. There was no lunch. It was-- was dinner. And then, from 3 o'clock till 8 o'clock-- winter-- we went to cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother make you for lunch? What special things did your mother cook?

speaker: interviewee
text: What-- first of all-- there was-- they called it snack. So in order to make sure that I eat it, they [LAUGHS] actually gave me an account in the bakery. I-- I-- went there, she gave me some [INAUDIBLE]. I buy on credit. And because some kids like better [NON-ENGLISH]-- you know what it means-- than the [INAUDIBLE], so she made sure that I had it there. This-- this was one way. And the other way, gave me a sandwich. It was a salami sandwich. Sometimes, in the winter time, it was [NON-ENGLISH], if you know what [NON-ENGLISH] is. So this was the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what special--

speaker: interviewee
text: And a fruit.

speaker: interviewer
text: What special things did she make at home that you remember that you loved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give me an idea of what your Shabbos table looked like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbos table, Friday night-- Friday night, we came home. Everybody has a seat. And my father was [NON-ENGLISH]. And he sang before [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. And then he says the [NON-ENGLISH] and whatever there was, a page of saying [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And he gave everybody a drink. And then it started [? seder. ?] [? Seder, ?] Friday night, [INAUDIBLE] was a-- a main-- a main course was fish. It was soup. It was meat. Usually sometimes my father got [NON-ENGLISH] and that. And we had-- [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what it means? Goose, goose meat, and then a compote. And this was everything. Did you have guests for Erev Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Not every week, but we had over.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did-- how did the guests come to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: From shtebl. They-- they-- they used to come to shtebl. And they waited till the end. And then, they dis--

speaker: interviewer
text: Distributed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Distributed people, he asked whoever wanted to take a order.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that way, did you meet people from other towns, when you were a young boy, that came to visit your town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poor people, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you give me an idea of a holiday? Did you ever have family that came to your house for holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to-- we had family in Chrzanów. So afternoon, they came to my mother. When I was young, they came. And she [SIGHS] had company. We went also to-- to-- to family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you have in town? Who was your family in town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My next family, we lived in a house when I was very young. We lived in a-- in-- in a street. I told you-- I mentioned it once-- it was [NON-ENGLISH], which is [INAUDIBLE], whatever. This is the name of that street. And in this house, we lived in one side. And mother's sister were on the other side of-- of the-- of the corridor. So she had also a sister. And we had cousins, [NON-ENGLISH]. They moved-- they-- they-- they luckily-- they moved to Israel before the war, because they moved from Chrzanów to Troppau, which was Sudeten Deutschland. And when-- when-- when the Germans came in, they-- they-- they left for Israel. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Zionists? Where they always Zionists?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody was a Zionist. Everybody was Zionist. They were religious people. And they had to leave. They didn't want to stay with the Germans. Maybe-- so they left to Israel. And there, they stood-- one sister is still there. One cousin, which is one-- the youngest from this family, is still alive. The rest of them is not here. They are not here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever think of moving to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: True-- truly my brother-in-law, sister's husband, once, he had a-- he had a tough time here in Chrzanów. He was [INAUDIBLE]. And he couldn't really make it out. So he thought-- it was just a thought-- to go to Israel, as a whatever. And he had a few dollars, whatever. But it-- it didn't realize.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever have any grandparents living with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chana Henda. My mother's mother. Chana Henda. She was-- she preferred to be with my mother than the aunt which was across, because she liked-- she liked better my father. So you know, son-in-law, he was-- he was a good-- a good son-in-law. And this was the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you learn from your grandmother? What are things that she told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [SIGHS] my grandmother had a-- had a-- had a hard life. Her husband died when he was 30 years. He left her with seven kids. He died. His name was Bendit, which my brother, his name-- my older brother's name is after this-- this grandfather. So what was the question?

speaker: interviewer
text: The things that you learned from your grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she-- she-- she talked about family at home, how-- how-- how she provided. And she-- she traveled from one-- from one-- from one son to the other son, to the other daughter. This was always-- only good things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a close relationship with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. As a matter of fact, [LAUGHS] sometimes she didn't like it. Because on a Saturday afternoon, [LAUGHS] I-- I-- I-- when she moved out with us, she was in a-- in a-- in a apartment from herself, I-- Shabbos, I bothered her when she was sleeping. I-- I went for-- for whatever this is. It used to be that-- that you go for something, like if something-- some goodies. So this was the way it was. But I was a kid. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do with your brother? Who were you closest with in your family, your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: With everybody, I fought. With Reuven, he was a year. He was-- I was born July, and he was born in December. So it was a year and a half. So we-- we were together. But we fought, too.

words.text: So it was a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you fight? What was fighting? Physical? Words?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I wanted to join his friends. And he didn't want it. So I started running. It was all-- this-- that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were they running to? What were they doing? What kind of--

speaker: interviewee
text: He had his friends, and I wanted to join his friends.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of games did they play? What did they do with themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kid stuff, I don't know-- played games, played-- played ball, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you good at sports?

speaker: interviewee
text: In school? I played, yeah. I did play.

speaker: interviewer
text: You enjoy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I enjoyed it. And then I played it also in cheder. We had-- we had big-- big enjoyment. In the winter, we had sled rides. And after-- after-- 8 o'clock, every-- everybody was running for his enjoyment. And we run between-- there was no-- sleds handmade. But it worked. And we had our enjoyment. We came home. We had to make the homework and go back to school. And this was-- this was the after day. That's-- that's how it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go away from your town, visit another town, when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. The only-- the only way, when I went away from my town, was-- the customary was everybody was-- had to register for-- for-- for-- for the military. This was by the year of-- by the age of 17, register. And then they called you in by-- by 21, by 20, 21, in this year, in this age. You had to stay for commission-- for commission. And it was customary the Jewish people didn't want to go to-- to-- to the army. And they looked for a way not to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when my turn came, I had to leave town. And I left home to Cracow. And I stood there, hidden. I didn't-- I didn't go to-- to-- to-- I didn't present myself. So I was illegal in Cracow. And my father sent me money, whatever, you know? Where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't hide. I didn't hide. I was in Cracow, with a-- with a-- with another friend, who also run from-- from Chrzanów. This was customary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where'd you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I-- got-- my father gave me money to rent-- to rent something. I slept in a-- in a-- in a house, where there were other people. So they gave me a bed. And then I slept there. And then I lived there. And this was going on until January, '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again, let me ask you the question, why didn't the Jews want to join the Polish army?

speaker: interviewee
text: Why? For two reasons. One reason was a Jew was very much [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] fragile about the kids, you know? They-- they didn't want to give them the rough-- rough time. And secondly, we weren't so much-- what do you call it-- citizens, good to Poland, that we should serve the army. They weren't so good to us that we should serve their army.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't feel Poland your homeland in any way?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my homeland. I was born there. I was educated. I went to school there and-- and-- and we had-- we had a business there. But [SIGHS] friendship-- there was no friendship. There was no friendship.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many Jews lived in your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: My hometown was-- it was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what do you call it-- a nice town. We had, I would say, 15,000, 20,000 Jews living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people in the whole town, do you know? Poles and Jews together?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were 2/3, 2/3. And we had-- we had-- I have a book here. You can see that-- that there was very much a social life, very much education-- education life. And everything was there, really everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a library?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a library. If you ask me a library in school, we had our-- our books. And-- and-- and in shtebl, there was a library that you wouldn't fit into this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Of the library.

speaker: interviewee
text: The name of it, [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH], if you know what it is. [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was a-- it was a lot going on. [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] There was still the-- it was a-- it was-- it was a library. You could study there 24 hours a day and-- and never-- never get tired of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have Jewish theater?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was sometimes came-- came a-- a troop to Chrzanów. [NON-ENGLISH]. It came. They came.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you saw some of it as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: As a child, not-- not-- not in a theater. I was--I was in a group-- we didn't go to theaters. But we went-- sometimes there was a Purim thing, or Hanukkah. And it was-- it was-- a group came to-- just to make-- make a party. So I went there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever do anything amongst your own students? Did you do something for Purim? Did you wear costumes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was customary. Yeah, customary-- we did [NON-ENGLISH]. We did all the things, which was-- we did even annoy people in [INAUDIBLE] to throw them those things which annoys them. I feel sorry for them, but that-- this was the custom. You know, this-- this-- those little [INAUDIBLE] in the beards? This was fun. But this-- this-- this was a part of-- of-- of the-- of the fun. But we did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you now-- you finished elementary school--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In this town. You-- at 17, you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: You-- elementary school before.

speaker: interviewer
text: You finished seven years of elementary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was-- it was 13. 11, 12, 13.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do between the time you finished elementary school and you went to Cracow? How-- what-- did you-- did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I studied. The most thing, I studied a few years in-- in the shtebl.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I belonged there. I belonged there. It was-- I learned [NON-ENGLISH] for a few years. Then I went to Sosnowice and [NON-ENGLISH], which I studied there for a year and a half.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay and sleep in the town in Sosnowice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yeah, I sleep in the town. And-- and some people-- ate [INAUDIBLE]. You know, [INAUDIBLE] One day here, one day here.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father-- I was in-- in a [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what is [NON-ENGLISH]? A-- a-- a place with a cook, where people went there every there. And the rabbi take-- took care of it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the reason was, my father was known to-- to rabbi's son-in-law. The son-in-law was-- was a-- was a son of-- of [INAUDIBLE]. And my father was-- was a-- was a [NON-ENGLISH]. If you know what it means. So he took me to the [NON-ENGLISH]. So I didn't [INAUDIBLE]. But we had boys in Chrzanów from the yeshiva, which they ate by us. One day, one, one-- another day, another one. This is the most customary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody helped each other within the community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew everybody in your community? You were all friendly?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I knew a lot of people. But I should tell you, if I take now the Chrzanów book, and I look up the people there, I didn't-- I didn't-- I didn't-- I didn't know many of them. I knew more-- more in my-- in my capacity where they were. There were, for example, organizations, [NON-ENGLISH]. They were-- there was a-- one was a Poliziani. There was-- one was a social-- a Bund, and this. I didn't belong there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I belonged more to the-- to the Haggadah, to the more religious ones. And that's what it was. So now, from this point, you went to Cracow. Your father is helping you in Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: We got you to Cracow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father is helping you, sending you money to rent a room.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he helped me out, and I should-- I should be able to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. We'll stop the tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue on the next tape.
